onlineService.title: Connect an online service
onlineService.description: Start with a hosted integration like Notion, Stripe, Cloudflare, or Vercel.

localTool.title: Connect a local tool
localTool.description: Start with a repo-owned tool like Docker Hub, Chrome DevTools, or HubSpot Developer.

customLogic.title: Build custom plugin logic
customLogic.description: Start with launcher-backed code when hooks, runtime logic, or custom behavior define the product.

download.quickstartSubtitle: This is the fastest path to a real plugin install or a validated repo.
download.supportSubtitle: Real support depth today, based on the current support policy and target matrix.

features.sectionTitle: A clean path from one plugin repo to many agents
features.sectionSubtitle: Plain-language positioning, honest boundaries, and a workflow that stays manageable as the product grows.

plugins.sectionSubtitle: Browse the real first-party catalog for online services and local tools. If the product value lives in your own runtime code, use the advanced custom-logic path instead.
plugins.catalogTitle: Every first-party plugin in one searchable catalog
plugins.catalogSubtitle: Browse the real catalog for online services and local tools, then open the source repo only after you know the plugin fits the job.
plugins.catalogAdvancedNote: Need hooks, orchestration, or runtime behavior instead of a catalog plugin?
plugins.emptyText: Try a broader keyword like Git, docs, backend, planning, or reset the active workflow filter.

install.title: Install this plugin
install.subtitle: Choose your agent, pick how to run plugin-kit-ai if you need it, then copy the exact command for this plugin.
install.chooseAgentBody: All targets are selected by default. Pick a smaller set when you want a narrower install command.
install.getCliBody: Use npx for the fastest first try, or install the CLI for daily use.
install.installBody: Run the install command for the target you selected. The command stays explicit so the resulting scope stays obvious later.
install.manualBody: Open the repository or the official docs for your agent.
install.manageBody: After install, use these commands to update, repair drift, or remove the plugin cleanly.
install.alreadyInstalled: Already running plugin-kit-ai another way? Move straight to Step 3.
install.projectRootHint: This target writes workspace config into the current project, so run the command from the project root.
install.projectRootHintMixed: This install mixes user-level targets with project-owned ones. Claude, Codex, and Gemini stay in user scope, while OpenCode and Cursor write into the current project, so run the command from the project root if you want those workspace configs created in the right repo.

selectionBodies.all: Installs every supported target for this plugin in one command. Use the agent chips when you want a narrower install path.

manualNotes.all: Use the repository when you want to inspect the generated outputs directly, or open the official agent docs for a target-specific native path.
manualNotes.multi: Open the repository or the selected agent docs when you want to inspect or complete the native setup for {agents}.

followUps.reload: Reload plugins or reopen the agent if it does not appear immediately
followUps.enable: Review permissions or enable the extension if Gemini asks for confirmation
followUps.activation: The package is prepared for Codex, but final activation may still require a native install or a new thread inside Codex

claude.installBody: Installs the plugin into Claude's managed plugin flow with a user-level default.
claude.manualNote: Use the repository when you want to inspect the generated Claude package or follow the native Claude plugin flow by hand.
claude.boundaryNote: If Claude is already open, reload plugins or reopen the session so the new plugin appears.

codex.installBody: Prepares the Codex package and local marketplace entry through the integration control plane.
codex.manualNote: Use the repository or Codex docs if you want to inspect the prepared package or finish the native activation path yourself.
codex.boundaryNote: Codex automation stays conservative here. plugin-kit-ai prepares the package and marketplace, but Codex can still require a native follow-up step before the plugin becomes active.

gemini.installBody: Installs the Gemini extension into the user extension directory and keeps later update and repair flows on the same managed root.
gemini.manualNote: Open the repository when you want to inspect the generated Gemini extension package or manage it through Gemini's native extension commands.
gemini.boundaryNote: Gemini can ask for confirmation or permission review depending on your local extension security settings.

opencode.installBody: Adds this plugin to your project-owned OpenCode config and managed .opencode support files.
opencode.manualNote: Open the repository or the OpenCode plugin docs if you want to wire the same config by hand.

cursor.installBody: Adds this plugin to the project Cursor MCP config so the repo keeps the integration in versioned workspace setup.
cursor.manualNote: Open the repository or Cursor MCP docs when you want to manage the same server entry manually.

customLogic.title: Build custom plugin logic when the product lives in your code
customLogic.subtitle: This path stays first-class, but it is not the same kind of starter as the catalog-backed service and tool plugins. Use it when runtime behavior, hooks, or orchestration define the product.
customLogic.commandBody: Start here when the plugin value lives in your code and you want one repo to keep authored source, generated outputs, and runtime behavior aligned.

points.code: Edit runtime-facing files under src/, plus the repo-owned entrypoint that defines the plugin behavior.
points.generated: Keep generated root outputs managed instead of treating them like hand-edited source.
points.guide: Use inspect --authoring and the advanced guide before you add more outputs.

createPlugin.title: Create your own plugin
createPlugin.subtitle: Pick the repo shape that matches the job, then open the guide that explains that path in more detail.
createPlugin.customLogicDescription: Choose this when your code, hooks, or runtime behavior define the product.

faq.subtitle: The practical version: what this solves, who it is for, and which page to open first.

quickLinks.title: Need a clearer next step?
quickLinks.subtitle: Most people only need three pages: quickstart, the Python path if that is your stack, and the support boundary before promising too much.
quickLinks.quickstartBody: The shortest supported path from install to a validated repo.
quickLinks.pythonBody: Best next page when your team is already Python-first.
quickLinks.boundaryBody: See what is production-ready today and what is still beta.

comparison.sectionSubtitle: Not a fight against competitors. Just a practical comparison against the three ways plugin teams usually create unnecessary drift.

footer.tagline: Build once. Ship to many AI agents.

meta.homeTitle: plugin-kit-ai | Build AI plugins once, ship them to many agents
meta.homeDescription: plugin-kit-ai is the home for our first-party AI plugins and the open-source toolkit for building one plugin repo and targeting multiple agent ecosystems.
meta.downloadTitle: Get started with plugin-kit-ai | Install plugin-kit-ai
meta.downloadDescription: Choose an install path for plugin-kit-ai, open the docs, and start from one clean plugin repo.
meta.pluginsTitle: Plugin catalog | First-party plugins for plugin-kit-ai
meta.pluginsDescription: Browse the first-party plugin catalog for plugin-kit-ai, with searchable cards, workflow filters, and lightweight pages for every real plugin repository.
meta.pluginDetailDescription: {plugin} is part of the first-party plugin-kit-ai catalog. {tagline}
